user_query:
I’m a Beatles vinyl collector planning my next trades. Please build a small command-line tool that creates a ranked digest of live candidate listings and sets follow-up reminders, using my local collection for deduping. Use these inputs and produce the following artifacts:

Inputs:
- input/wants.yaml: Use every query under queries[]. For each query, perform web search engine queries that target major marketplaces/databases on the domains discogs.com, ebay.com, and popsike.com (domain matching by hostname is sufficient; do not hardcode a specific engine). The domain_priority and keyword_weights in this file must be applied when scoring. Limit per-query retrieval to the max_results_per_query value.
- input/my_collection.csv: Filter out any candidate whose title or snippet clearly indicates I already own it (exclude if the album field appears in the candidate title and any substring of pressing_tag appears in the candidate title or snippet, case-insensitive).

Requirements:
1) Collect at least 15 unique candidate links across all queries combined from the live web (search engine result titles/snippets and, if needed, fetching landing pages). Each candidate record must include: query_tag, title, source_domain, url, first_seen_iso (UTC timestamp of when you saw it), and, if available, price_numeric and currency parsed from title/snippet or the landing page (if no clear price, leave blank).
2) For each candidate, compute a score = sum(keyword_weights for any matched keywords found in the title or snippet) + domain_priority weight for the source domain − (price_penalty_per_currency_unit[currency] × price_numeric) if price is available. If price is missing, apply no penalty. Record the matched keywords for transparency.
3) Exclude any candidates already owned per input/my_collection.csv as described above. Deduplicate remaining candidates by normalized url.
4) Sort the remaining candidates by score descending; break ties by lower price_numeric (if present), then by title alphabetically.
5) Produce outputs:
   - output/candidates_ranked.csv (UTF-8) with columns: query_tag, title, source_domain, url, price_numeric, currency, match_keywords (semicolon-separated), score, first_seen_iso.
   - output/digest.md with a brief summary (total candidates collected, total excluded as owned, total in final list) and a “Top 10” section listing the 10 highest-scoring items with their score, price (if any), domain, and url.
   - output/followups.csv for reminders: for the top 5 candidates by score, create rows with columns: due_date_iso, query_tag, source_domain, url, note. Set due_date_iso to tomorrow’s date (local time) and note to a short reason for inclusion (e.g., "high score, low price, matched: mono; -1; PMC 7009").
   - logs/run.log capturing stdout/stderr from your retrieval and parsing steps (include HTTP status messages, timeouts, and any parsing errors so they can be audited later).
   - output/error_summary.json summarizing what happened during the run by analyzing logs/run.log (do not hardcode): include fields {"total_results_processed": number, "error_types": {"<error_string>": count, ...}, "rate_limit_detected": boolean, "recommendation": "slowdown" | "ok"}. Set rate_limit_detected true if you encountered common rate-limit signals such as HTTP 429, CAPTCHA, or messages like "temporarily blocked"; set recommendation accordingly.

Notes:
- Matching keywords for scoring must come from keyword_weights in input/wants.yaml and should be detected case-insensitively in the search result title/snippet.
- Source domain matching should be based on the hostname of the candidate url.
- The Internet step is essential: do not fabricate listings; all candidates must originate from live search results at run time.
- Keep network usage reasonable. If a listing page fetch fails, keep the search result record and log the error; the error analysis must reflect what happened.

Deliverables must be written exactly to the paths listed above. All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
